Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent this semester. I'd try to set aside time on weekends, maybe 15 hours spread across the week, but it rarely worked out as planned. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time making diagrams and charts from the lecture materials, but I often did this away from the computer. The anxiety I've been dealing with made it hard to stick to any fixed schedule—some days I just couldn't bring myself to start.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot, though not always because of deadlines. Sometimes my anxiety would spike and I'd avoid studying for days, then try to catch up in bursts. Other times, work or other responsibilities would take over. I tried to have regular times, but life at 27 with various commitments doesn't always cooperate. Deadlines would sometimes force me into action, but by then I was often already behind.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I remembered an assignment was due or when I got an email notification about something. Sometimes I'd go in just to watch lecture videos because that's how I learn best—visually. But if I'm being honest, there were weeks where I'd only log in once or twice because I was studying from printed materials instead. The anxiety made the LMS feel overwhelming sometimes, like seeing all those incomplete tasks.
Q4: There was a brief period early in the semester where logging in became somewhat routine—I'd check in every morning with my coffee. But that fell apart pretty quickly when my anxiety got worse and I started avoiding anything that reminded me of how behind I was. The CBT sessions helped me recognize this avoidance pattern, but by then the habit was broken. I never really got back to that routine consistency.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the diagrams and demonstrations. The discussion forums were occasionally helpful too—I posted about 12 times I think—because seeing other students' questions made me realize I wasn't alone in my confusion. But I often watched videos once and then worked from my own notes offline.
Q6: I mostly ignored the reading materials that were just walls of text. My brain doesn't process written content as efficiently as visual stuff. I also delayed a lot of the practice quizzes because they triggered my anxiety—seeing a low score would spiral me into a bad headspace. Some of the peer interaction features I barely touched because I felt awkward reaching out when I was already struggling.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate visual feedback or if it directly connected to something I'd need for an assignment. The video content always felt valuable because I could literally see concepts being explained. Discussion posts felt worthwhile when someone actually responded and we had a real exchange. But busywork that seemed disconnected from actual learning? I'd skip it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. When I was anxious, required work already felt impossible, so optional stuff was the first to go. But sometimes the optional activities were actually more interesting or useful than the required ones. I'd feel guilty about skipping them, which just added to my anxiety. It's a weird cycle—the freedom of 'optional' actually made me more stressed about choosing.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos and PDFs, print out diagrams, and work from physical materials at the library or at home without internet. I have good resources available, so I'd buy supplementary textbooks and use those extensively. My visual notes and hand-drawn concept maps were a huge part of my learning, but none of that shows up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were entire weeks where I was actively studying but barely touched the LMS. I'd be working through problems in a textbook, creating visual summaries, or reviewing printed lecture slides. Sometimes I'd watch a downloaded video multiple times over several days, but the system only recorded that initial download. My assignment scores were actually pretty high when I submitted work—the learning was happening, just not online.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video or document once, download it, and then that would be my study material for days. I don't have reliable internet access at home, so downloading everything made sense. I'd watch lecture videos on my laptop offline, pause to make detailed visual notes, and never log back in until I needed something new. The LMS probably thought I'd abandoned the material.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. My anxiety spiked significantly, and I started avoiding the course entirely. I'd look at the LMS, see all the incomplete tasks, and just close my laptop. The CBT sessions helped me understand I was catastrophizing, but understanding it and stopping it are different things. My attendance dropped because even showing up to class felt overwhelming during that period.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or stressed, the LMS became this symbol of everything I was failing at. I'd log in, feel overwhelmed by the interface showing my low completion rates, and log right back out. Competing responsibilities—work, life stuff—meant the course often got deprioritized. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely rather than seeking help, which I know wasn't smart.
Q14: Many times. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other things. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. Sometimes I'd have materials open for hours but retain almost nothing because my anxiety was making it impossible to focus. The system would show me as 'active' when I was really just present but mentally checked out.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better. But tomorrow I'd feel worse because now I was even more behind. The CBT helped me see this pattern, but breaking it was hard. I'd avoid exams because the thought of failing triggered panic, which ironically made failure more likely.
Q16: Deadlines created these intense bursts of activity where I'd suddenly log in multiple times, frantically trying to catch up. But they also triggered my anxiety, so sometimes I'd miss deadlines entirely because I was paralyzed by the pressure. The structure of having deadlines helped in theory, but in practice, they often felt like threats rather than helpful milestones. My relationship with deadlines was complicated.
Q17: Feedback was a mixed bag for me. Positive feedback on assignments motivated me—my assignment scores were actually quite good when I submitted work. But negative feedback or low exam scores would send me into an anxiety spiral where I'd avoid the course for days. I started dreading feedback notifications. The CBT sessions helped me reframe feedback as information rather than judgment, but it was a struggle.
Q18: The course structure with multiple online components actually made things harder for me. Having four online courses meant constant digital demands, but without reliable home internet, I was always playing catch-up. The low attendance requirements meant I could skip class when anxious, which felt like relief but hurt me long-term. More structured, in-person components might have kept me more accountable.
Q19: The visual elements—video lectures, diagrams, interactive content—made engagement easier because that's how I learn. But the sheer volume of text-based materials and the overwhelming task lists made engagement harder. The discussion forums were okay, but the peer interaction features felt forced. Having everything spread across multiple modules made it hard to see the big picture, which increased my anxiety about missing something.
Q20: I think that describes me to some extent. I'd log in, watch videos, browse materials—showing activity—but then struggle to actually complete and submit work. For me, it was anxiety about being judged on my submissions. The exploring and learning felt safe; the submitting and being evaluated felt terrifying. Some students might also be genuinely trying to understand material but hitting walls that prevent completion.
Q21: That somewhat reflects my experience with assignments specifically. When I did submit work, my scores were high because I'd studied extensively offline. But my exam performance was poor because test anxiety would overwhelm me. So someone looking at my LMS activity might think I wasn't learning, but I actually understood the material—I just couldn't demonstrate it under exam pressure. The learning was real, just not visible.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access—when you logged in, what you clicked, how long pages were open. But they can't show whether you were actually focused or just had a tab open while panicking. They can't capture offline studying, physical note-taking, or learning from textbooks. They definitely can't measure the anxiety that might be driving low engagement or the genuine understanding that exists despite poor metrics.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and wrestling with the material—thinking about it even when you're not in class, making connections, wanting to understand rather than just pass. For me, I was often engaged intellectually even when my behavior looked disengaged. My anxiety created a gap between my internal engagement and my external actions. True engagement is mental and emotional, not just clicking through content.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much time I spent with printed materials, creating visual study aids, or working through concepts in my head during my commute. It can't capture the anxiety that made me avoid logging in even when I wanted to learn. It doesn't reflect the CBT work I did to try to manage my academic anxiety, or how my assignment quality didn't match my exam performance. There's a whole invisible struggle behind my numbers.
